summary: A 10th-century Old English manuscript (codex) found in Vercelli, Italy. Contains The Dream of the Rood, alliterative poems by Cynewulf (including Elene and The Fates of the Apostles), and a collection of prose homilies. One of the four major codices of Old English literature.

works.name: The Dream of the Rood
works.description: The most-clued text in the Vercelli Book. An Old English poem in alliterative verse in which the narrator has a vision of the Cross (the 'rood'), which then narrates Christ's crucifixion from its own perspective. The cross is depicted as covered in gems and gold, and as bleeding on its right side. Variously attributed to Caedmon or Cynewulf. Part of the poem is also inscribed in runic characters on the 8th-century Ruthwell Cross in Scotland. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Cross narrates Christ's crucifixion from its own perspective; cross is covered in gems and gold, bleeds on right side
examples: In a poem about the 'best of dreams' collected in the Vercelli Codex, a jewel-encrusted object that was central to this event recounts the story of its own creation.
That poem is about an object that begins bleeding on the right side and is bedecked with gems and gold.

clues.clue: Preserved (partly) on the Ruthwell Cross — a stone cross in a Scottish border town — predating any manuscript
examples: The first printed version of a poem about this event antedates a version carved onto a stone monument in Ruthwell.

Predating any Old English manuscripts, this large eighth-century stone cross, named for the Scottish border town where it still stands, is believed to independently preserve part of The Dream of the Rood.

clues.clue: Attributed variously to Caedmon or Cynewulf; attribution to Caedmon based on circumstantial evidence in Bede's Ecclesiastical History
examples: Variously attributed to Caedmon and Cynewulf, this poem is primarily preserved in the Vercelli Codex.
The attribution of the poem to Caedmon is based primarily on circumstantial evidence in the Ecclesiastical History of the English People, a work by this 'venerable' monk of Jarrow.

clues.clue: Written in alliterative verse (Old English poetic meter)
examples: Name this verse form used to write The Dream of the Rood and most other Old English poetry.
The Dream of the Rood uses this device to underpin its poetic meter. Beowulf also uses this device.

clues.clue: Opens with narrator's vision of the Cross ('best of dreams'); first section is the narrator's vision, second is the cross speaking
examples: Its first section describes the narrator's vision of the Cross, while its second part describes Christ's crucifixion from the perspective of the cross itself.
In a poem titled for this phenomenon from the Vercelli Codex, an inanimate wooden object praises God after narrating how it was pierced with nails.

clues.clue: One of the oldest surviving Old English poems
examples: thought to be one of the oldest surviving in the Old English corpus

clues.clue: Complete text discovered in 1822 as part of the Vercelli Book
examples: The complete text of this poem was discovered in 1822 as part of the 10th century Vercelli Book.

works.name: Cynewulf's Poems (Elene, The Fates of the Apostles, etc.)
works.description: The Vercelli Book also contains alliterative poems signed by Cynewulf, one of the few named Old English poets. Included are Elene, in which a character is renamed Cyriacus after finding three crosses underground, and The Fates of the Apostles. Cynewulf also wrote Juliana and Christ II (in the Exeter Book). Wikipedia

examples: In the poem Elene, a character is renamed Cyriacus after finding three of these objects underground.
In a poem sometimes titled for one of these objects, a character has his name changed after helping the protagonist dig up three of these objects.

clues.clue: Cynewulf is one of the few named Old English authors; he signed his poems (Juliana, Christ II, Elene, Fates of the Apostles) using runic letters
examples: He is one of the only Old English authors whose name is known, as he signed his poems Juliana and Christ II.
The Vercelli Book also includes this author's poems The Fates of the Apostles and Elene.

works.name: General / Codex Identity
works.description: Identification clues for the Vercelli Book as a whole — a 10th-century codex named for the Italian city of Vercelli where it is held. Contains The Dream of the Rood, Cynewulf's alliterative poems, and prose homilies. One of four major Old English codices (alongside the Exeter Book, the Nowell Codex, and the Junius Manuscript). Wikipedia

comprehensive_summary:
The Vercelli Book (also called the Vercelli Codex) is a 10th-century manuscript of Old English literature, named for the Italian city of Vercelli where it is held. It is one of the four major surviving codices of Old English writing, alongside the Exeter Book, the Nowell Codex (which contains Beowulf), and the Junius Manuscript.

The codex's most-clued contents are The Dream of the Rood, alliterative poems by Cynewulf, and a collection of prose homilies. The Dream of the Rood is considered one of the oldest surviving Old English poems and is thought to be one of the finest examples of Old English alliterative verse. In this poem, the narrator recounts a 'best of dreams' vision of the Cross (the rood), which then narrates the crucifixion of Christ from its own perspective — describing how it was forced to participate, how it bled on the right side, and how it was adorned with gems and gold. The poem is in two main sections: the narrator's vision, then the cross's first-person account. Authorship is attributed variously to Caedmon or Cynewulf; the attribution to Caedmon rests on circumstantial evidence in Bede's Ecclesiastical History of the English People. An early version of the poem — predating the Vercelli Book manuscript — is inscribed in runic characters on the Ruthwell Cross, an 8th-century stone monument in a Scottish border town. The complete text was discovered in 1822.

Cynewulf is one of the only named Old English poets, having signed his works using runic letters woven into the text. His poems in the Vercelli Book include Elene, in which Helena's discovery of the True Cross is dramatized and a character is renamed Cyriacus after finding three crosses underground, and The Fates of the Apostles. His other known works (in the Exeter Book) include Juliana and Christ II.

In quizbowl, the Vercelli Book most frequently appears as a bonus part answer in the context of Old English literature questions, particularly those about The Dream of the Rood and alliterative verse. The giveaway identification is: an Old English codex named for an Italian city that contains The Dream of the Rood alongside Cynewulf's poems and prose homilies. W.H. Auden's long poem The Age of Anxiety uses a modified form of alliterative verse — the same verse form used in The Dream of the Rood and most Old English poetry.
recursive_suggestions: The Dream of the Rood — appears as its own answerline in many quizbowl questions; deserves dedicated entry
Cynewulf — named Old English poet whose works appear in both the Vercelli Book and the Exeter Book; may have answerline clues
Ruthwell Cross — frequently clued in connection with Dream of the Rood; could have its own clue set
Exeter Book — the other major Old English codex, often compared; contains Beowulf, riddles, etc.
Alliterative verse — appears as its own answerline in at least one question; Old English poetic form
